## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)

**Which flow and why:** `send` fanning out — it touches every class and shows the Mediator, Strategy, and Observer all firing at runtime.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor Alice
    participant S as ChatServer
    participant R as ChatRoom
    participant D as DeliveryStrategy
    participant Bob
    participant Carol

    Alice->>S: send(general, u1, hi)
    S->>S: _require_room + _require_known_user
    rect rgb(40, 60, 90)
    Note over S,R: critical section under per-room lock
    S->>R: accept(u1, hi)
    R->>R: build Message, append to history
    R->>D: select_recipients(general, u1, members)
    D-->>R: Bob and Carol
    R-->>S: message plus recipients
    end
    S->>Bob: receive(message)
    S->>Carol: receive(message)
    S-->>Alice: return message
```

> 🎙️ **Walk it arrow by arrow:** "Alice calls `send` on the `ChatServer`. The server doesn't do the work — it first validates: the room exists, and Alice is a known user. Then, *under the room lock*, it calls `accept` on the `ChatRoom` — the mediator. The room builds the `Message`, appends it to history, and here's the interesting moment: it calls `select_recipients` on the `DeliveryStrategy` — that's where the Strategy fires. The strategy returns `[Bob, Carol]` (Alice excluded). The room hands `(message, recipients)` back and the lock releases. *Now*, outside the lock, the server fires the Observer callback — `receive` — on Bob and Carol. Finally it returns the message to Alice."

> **Why it shows the design is good:** "Notice the server coordinates but implements no business logic — it delegates *who gets it* to the strategy and *how it's recorded* to the room, and it does the slow fan-out outside the lock. That's Open/Closed and the snapshot-then-deliver concurrency pattern in one picture."

## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility

**DEFAULT: describe the solution. Write code only if asked.**

🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact race:** "The race is in `send` vs `join`/`leave` on the same room. `send` reads `_members` to snapshot recipients; a concurrent `join` mutates that same dict. Two problems: the snapshot can iterate a dict that's resizing (a `RuntimeError`), or a message can miss a just-joined member / hit a just-left one. It's a read-write race plus check-then-act on shared state."

🗣️ **Step 2 — The solution:** "One lock per room. `send`, `join`, `leave`, and `history` all acquire that room's lock around the part that touches `_members`/`_history`. Now record-and-snapshot is atomic with membership changes."

🗣️ **Step 3 — The trade-off:** "Per-room locking (not one global lock) means ten different rooms never block each other — only members of the *same* room serialize, for a microsecond. And I keep the fan-out (`receive`) *outside* the lock, so a slow receiver never stalls the room."

🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed:** "Across servers an in-process lock is useless — each process has its own memory. I'd move membership + history to a store and make the fan-out a message bus (publish to a per-room topic; each server delivers to its locally-connected members). Ordering becomes the store's job — a per-room sequence number or a log like Kafka partitioned by room id."

**Concurrency Vocabulary**

| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
|---|---|---|
| **Race condition** | Two threads touch shared state at once; result depends on timing | `send` snapshot vs `join` mutating `_members` |
| **Critical section** | The block that must run as one atomic step | `ChatRoom.accept` — validate + record + snapshot |
| **Check-then-act** | Check a condition, then act, but state changed between | "saw member, then snapshotted" while a leave runs |
| **Atomic** | All-or-nothing; no half-done state observable | what the per-room lock gives `accept` |
| **Mutex / lock** | A token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.RLock` per room |
| **Reentrant (RLock)** | Same thread can re-acquire a lock it holds | the per-room lock; chosen so a guarded method can safely call another guarded helper (defensive — ops don't nest today) |
| **Lock granularity** | How much one lock covers — coarse vs fine | we chose per-ROOM, not one global lock |
| **Pessimistic locking** | Grab the lock before touching state | the in-process per-room `RLock` |
| **Optimistic locking** | Don't lock; write conditionally, retry on conflict | the distributed compare-and-set on membership/sequence |
| **Compare-and-set** | Atomic "update only if value is still X" | the DB `WHERE seq = ?` on the per-room log |
| **Deadlock** | Two threads each hold a lock the other needs | avoided: we ever hold only ONE lock at a time |
| **Starvation** | A thread never gets the lock | not a risk: the lock is held microseconds |

**Concurrency Follow-up Q&A**

> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
> "Defensive reentrancy. Today no guarded method nests inside another, so a plain `Lock` would work. But the moment a guarded op needs to call another guarded helper — say `leave` posting a 'X left' system message through the same locked path — a plain `Lock` would deadlock on the second acquire by the same thread. `RLock` lets the owning thread re-enter and only fully releases at the outermost `with`. It costs nothing and future-proofs that."

> ❓ "Why per-room locks, not one global lock?"
> "A global lock serializes *every* room through one bottleneck even though rooms share nothing. Per-room locking lets thousands of rooms operate in parallel; only members of the same room serialize, and that section is microseconds. I also keep `receive` outside the lock so a slow delivery never widens the critical section."

> ❓ "Why deliver outside the lock — isn't that a race?"
> "No. Inside the lock I take a *snapshot* of recipients and append the message to history. Delivery just calls `receive` (an atomic list append) on each snapshot member. The snapshot is immutable for this send, so a later join/leave can't affect it, and the appends don't touch any shared room state. Worst case a member who left a microsecond ago still gets this one in-flight message — acceptable and consistent with 'they were a member when it was sent'."

> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
> "None by construction. We only ever hold one lock at a time — the per-room lock — and all IO/fan-out is outside it. There's no lock-ordering cycle. The one possible re-entry is the same thread re-taking the same room lock, which `RLock` handles."

> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is tiny and same-room contention is low, so a short-held lock with no retry loop is simplest. Distributed, I flip to optimistic — you can't hold a Python lock across servers, so membership/ordering becomes a conditional DB write (compare-and-set on a per-room sequence) with retry."

> ❓ "The global `_users` directory is written on every join — is that a race?"
> "Dict set-item is atomic under the GIL, so concurrent joins to *different* rooms writing `_users` won't corrupt it. If I wanted strict cross-room safety beyond GIL guarantees I'd guard `_users` with a small dedicated lock — but the hot, contended path is per-room, which is already locked."

❓ **IF ASKED — the thread-safe version. Two things:**

**Part A — the no-op hook + subclass.** The clean `ChatServer._lock` is a no-op `@contextmanager` (zero lock code in the core). `ThreadSafeChatServer` overrides only `_lock`, using the **double-lock idiom**: a small guard lock protects the *dict of per-room locks*, then we hold the per-room `RLock` for the work. `RLock` (not `Lock`) so a guarded method may call another guarded helper; per-room (not global) so different rooms run in parallel.

**Part B — the exact modified method (no abbreviations):**
```python
# ─── §6 version (clean, single-threaded) ───────────────────────────────────
def send(self, room_id: str, sender_id: str, text: str) -> Message:
    room = self._require_room(room_id)
    self._require_known_user(sender_id)
    with self._lock(room):                       # no-op hook in the clean core
        message, recipients = room.accept(sender_id, text)
    for member in recipients:
        member.receive(message)
    return message

# ─── §9 version (thread-safe) — diff: ZERO lines in send; only _lock changes ─
class ThreadSafeChatServer(ChatServer):
    def __init__(self, delivery=None, now_fn=time.time):
        super().__init__(delivery, now_fn)
        self._room_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
        self._locks_guard = threading.Lock()     # guards the dict of locks

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, room):
        with self._locks_guard:                  # 1) lock the map of locks
            lock = self._room_locks.get(room.room_id)
            if lock is None:
                lock = threading.RLock()
                self._room_locks[room.room_id] = lock
        with lock:                               # 2) hold the per-room lock
            yield
```

> 🎙️ "The beautiful part: `send` itself doesn't change at all. The `with self._lock(room)` was already there as a no-op in §6; the subclass just gives that hook a real per-room `RLock`. The diff from clean to thread-safe is one overridden method — that's the Template Method paying off."

**Part C — the proof (Barrier test, already run):** `test_concurrent_sends_lose_no_messages` — 40 members race into one room at one instant; every member ends with exactly the 39 others' messages, history holds all 40, all ids unique, zero errors. A second test races joins *and* sends together and asserts history is exactly N with unique ids — proving a concurrent join during broadcast stays consistent.

**Edge cases handled**

- Send by non-member → `NotAMemberError` → `test_send_by_non_member_raises`.
- Send by unseen user → `UnknownUserError` → `test_send_by_unknown_user_raises`.
- Join unknown room → `UnknownRoomError` → `test_join_unknown_room_raises`.
- Duplicate join → `AlreadyMemberError` → `test_duplicate_join_raises`.
- Leave non-member → `NotAMemberError` → `test_leave_non_member_raises`.
- Sender never echoed → empty sender inbox → `test_join_then_send_delivers_to_others_only`.
- History is a copy → external append doesn't leak → `test_history_is_ordered_and_copied`.
- Muted member skipped → `test_muted_member_gets_nothing_end_to_end`.
- Concurrent sends/joins → no loss/dup/corruption → the two Barrier tests.

**(iii) Twist walkthroughs**

> **Twist 1 — "Only deliver to members who are currently online."**
>
> Right now we deliver to every member (minus muted). Online-ness is a new fan-out rule.

> **Twist 2 — "Persist history so it survives a restart."**

>     ...
> ```
> **Trade-off:** Adds a storage dependency and a latency hit per send; worth it for durability, overkill if history can be ephemeral.

> **Twist 3 — "Post a 'Alice joined' system message when someone joins."**
>
> Joins are silent today. We want a synthetic message in history + fan-out — and this is where reentrancy earns its keep.
>
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "I add a `_post_system(room, text)` that builds a sender-less system message through the same locked path `send` uses. Because `join` already holds the room lock and `_post_system` re-enters it, the `RLock` makes that safe with zero extra code."
>
> **The change:**
> ```python
> def join(self, room_id, user):
>     room = self._require_room(room_id)
>     with self._lock(room):
>         room.add_member(user)
>         msg, recipients = room.accept_system(f"{user.name} joined")
>     self._users[user.id] = user
>     for m in recipients:
>         m.receive(msg)
> ```
> **Trade-off:** `Message` needs a nullable `sender_id` (or a `SYSTEM` sentinel); small model change, and now the `RLock` reentrancy is genuinely used, not just defensive.

**Hardest follow-up questions**

> ❓ "What's the hardest concurrency bug hiding here, and how do you kill it?"
> "A message delivered to a member who joined *after* the snapshot, or missed by one who joined just before. The fix is the snapshot-under-lock in `accept`: membership mutation (`add_member`) and recipient snapshot serialize on the same per-room `RLock`, so for any given message a join is strictly before (included) or after (excluded) — never a torn read. The 'joins during broadcast' Barrier test proves history stays exactly N with unique ids."

> ❓ "What if you have multiple app servers?"
> "In-process locks don't span processes. I'd externalize state: membership + history in a shared store, and fan-out via a message bus — publish each message to a per-room topic; every server delivers to the members connected to *it*. The atomic step (record + sequence) moves to the store as a compare-and-set on a per-room sequence number; ordering is the log's job."

> ❓ "What if a room has 10 million members (a broadcast channel)?"
> "Synchronous fan-out to 10M inboxes is O(m) on the send path — unacceptable. I'd flip the model: don't push to per-user inboxes; write the message once to the room's log and let clients *pull* (fan-out-on-read), or tier the fan-out through a hierarchy of relays. The Strategy seam helps — `select_recipients` becomes 'who to notify to come pull' rather than 'who to copy to'."

> ❓ "What if delivery fails halfway through the recipient list?"
> "Today `receive` is an in-memory append that can't fail. With real delivery (sockets), I'd make the room's history the source of truth and delivery best-effort + idempotent: each client tracks the last message id it has and reconciles by pulling history on reconnect. Because `Message` is immutable and ordered, redelivery is safe — the client dedups by id. So a partial fan-out is self-healing, not a lost message."

> 🎙️ **30-second summary:** "A group chat is a decoupling problem: the room is a Mediator so N members never wire to each other, delivery is an Observer callback, and *who* gets a message is a Strategy — broadcast or mute-aware, a new rule is a new class with zero core changes. The one race is `send` snapshotting members while a `join` mutates them; I guard it with a per-room `RLock` via a Template-Method `_lock` hook — snapshot under the lock, fan out outside it — proved by a 40-thread Barrier test where every member receives exactly the others' messages. For multi-server scale I push the atomic record to a per-room log with compare-and-set and fan out over a message bus."

View problem statement
Problem statement
Design the core of a group-chat system. Users join chat rooms and send messages; every message is delivered to all other members of the room. Members must not be coupled directly to one another.

Core Requirements:

Rooms & Membership: create rooms; users join and leave.
Send: a member sends a message to a room; it is delivered to all other members (not echoed to the sender).
History: each room keeps an ordered message history.
Delivery Policy: how a message fans out should be pluggable (broadcast-to-all, skip-muted).
Validation: reject unknown rooms/users, sending by a non-member, and duplicate joins.

Additional Considerations:

No member-to-member coupling: members know only the room (Mediator); delivery is via an observer callback.
Thread-safety under concurrent sends and joins in the same room.

Design Considerations:

Use Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles.
Identify key classes (e.g. ChatServer, ChatRoom, User, Message, DeliveryStrategy).
Use the Mediator pattern (room mediates members) plus Observer (members receive).
Discuss design patterns (Mediator, Observer, Strategy for delivery, Template Method for the lock hook).
Focus on decoupling members and on per-room thread-safety.

The orchestrator validates at the boundary with layered errors: unknown room, then unknown user, then not-a-member. Concurrency is the real teeth here — a per-room RLock via a Template-Method _lock hook, snapshot recipients under the lock, fan out the callbacks outside it so a slow receiver never blocks a sender. # Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the Group Chat / Chat Room system.

The two teaching patterns live here in seed form:
  - `User` is the OBSERVER side — every member exposes one callback, `receive`,
    and the room pushes messages into it. Members never call each other.
  - `Message` is a frozen value object — once a message is stamped and recorded
    it can never change, so history is trustworthy and safe to share.

The room itself (the MEDIATOR) lives in `chat_service.py`; it is the only thing
that ever touches a member, so members stay completely ignorant of one another.

🎙️ "The whole point of this design is decoupling: N members never wire up to
each other (that's N-squared links). Each member knows only the room, the room
knows the members, and delivery is a one-method observer callback. Adding or
removing a member is O(1) and touches nobody else."
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field


# ── EXCEPTIONS (one base, one subclass per failure mode) ─────────────────────
class ChatError(Exception):
    """Base for every error this system raises — callers catch one type."""


class UnknownRoomError(ChatError):
    """A room id that was never created was passed to a public method."""


class UnknownUserError(ChatError):
    """A sender id the server has never seen (nobody by that id ever joined)."""


class NotAMemberError(ChatError):
    """A known user acted on a room they are not currently a member of."""


class AlreadyMemberError(ChatError):
    """A user tried to join a room they are already a member of."""


# ── MESSAGE: the frozen value object ─────────────────────────────────────────
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Message:
    """One chat message. Frozen on purpose: once the room stamps and records it,
    nothing — not a retry, not a late observer — can mutate it.

    🎙️ "A Message is a value object. It carries who sent it, which room, the
    text, and a timestamp. I freeze it so the same object can sit in the room's
    history AND in every member's inbox with zero risk of one reader mutating
    what another reader sees."
    """

    id: str
    sender_id: str
    room_id: str
    text: str
    timestamp: float


# ── USER: the member / observer ──────────────────────────────────────────────
@dataclass
class User:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # A member of zero or more rooms and the OBSERVER side of the design. The
    # room delivers a message by calling exactly one method on it — `receive`.
    # The user never reaches into another user; it only ever sees its own inbox.
    id: str
    name: str
    inbox: list[Message] = field(default_factory=list)
    muted_rooms: set[str] = field(default_factory=set)

    def receive(self, message: Message) -> None:
        # WHY: this is the single observer callback the room fires on delivery.
        #      A plain append — list.append is atomic in CPython, so concurrent
        #      deliveries from several senders all land with no lost messages.
        self.inbox.append(message)

    def mute(self, room_id: str) -> None:
        # WHY: muting is a per-USER preference, so it lives on the user, not the
        #      room. The MutedAwareDelivery strategy reads this set to skip me.
        self.muted_rooms.add(room_id)

    def unmute(self, room_id: str) -> None:
        self.muted_rooms.discard(room_id)

# Python 3.11+
"""Delivery strategies — the seam that decides WHO a room's message fans out to.

The room (the mediator) never hardcodes "everyone but the sender". It asks a
DeliveryStrategy for the recipient list, then fires the observer callback on each.
Swapping the fan-out policy (broadcast vs mute-aware vs, later, "only online")
is a new class with zero room changes.

🎙️ "The mediator owns membership and history, but it delegates the fan-out
policy to a Strategy. Today it's broadcast-to-everyone; tomorrow it's skip-muted
or skip-offline — each is one new class, the room doesn't change."
"""

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

from models import User


class DeliveryStrategy(ABC):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The abstraction the room depends on (Dependency Inversion). Given the
    # sender and the room's current members, it returns exactly who should get
    # the message. EVERY strategy must honor one rule: never echo to the sender.
    @abstractmethod
    def select_recipients(
        self, room_id: str, sender_id: str, members: list[User]
    ) -> list[User]:
        """Return the members who should receive a message from `sender_id`."""
        raise NotImplementedError


class BroadcastToAll(DeliveryStrategy):
    """Default: deliver to every member except the sender."""

    def select_recipients(
        self, room_id: str, sender_id: str, members: list[User]
    ) -> list[User]:
        # WHY: the `m.id != sender_id` filter is the "not echoed to sender" rule
        #      that EVERY strategy must keep — it lives in one place per policy.
        return [m for m in members if m.id != sender_id]


class MutedAwareDelivery(DeliveryStrategy):
    """Skip members who have muted this room (and never the sender)."""

    def select_recipients(
        self, room_id: str, sender_id: str, members: list[User]
    ) -> list[User]:
        # WHY: mute is a per-user preference (User.muted_rooms), so the strategy
        #      reads it off each candidate — the room stores no mute state at all.
        return [
            m
            for m in members
            if m.id != sender_id and room_id not in m.muted_rooms
        ]

# Python 3.11+
"""ChatRoom — the MEDIATOR — and ChatServer — the orchestrator.

The room is the only object that ever touches a member, so members are fully
decoupled from one another (no N-squared wiring). The server is the public API:
it owns the rooms map and the global user directory, and it wraps every room
mutation in a `_lock(room)` hook that does nothing in the clean core and becomes
a real per-room RLock in `ThreadSafeChatServer`.

🎙️ "Members never talk to each other — they talk to the room. The room records
the message and asks the delivery strategy who should get it. The orchestrator
sits on top, validates the request, and runs the fan-out outside the lock."
"""

import itertools
import threading
import time
from collections.abc import Callable, Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager

from models import (
    AlreadyMemberError,
    Message,
    NotAMemberError,
    UnknownRoomError,
    UnknownUserError,
    User,
)
from strategies import BroadcastToAll, DeliveryStrategy


class ChatRoom:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The MEDIATOR. It owns its members, its ordered history, and its delivery
    # policy. Members are wired ONLY to the room, never to each other, so adding
    # or removing one is O(1) and invisible to everyone else. The room is the
    # single place a message is stamped, recorded, and routed.
    # 🎙️ "This is the mediator. Instead of every member holding references to
    #     every other member, each member holds one reference — the room — and
    #     the room holds the members. That collapses N-squared links into N."
    def __init__(
        self,
        room_id: str,
        delivery: DeliveryStrategy,
        now_fn: Callable[[], float],
        id_fn: Callable[[], str],
    ) -> None:
        self.room_id = room_id
        self._members: dict[str, User] = {}     # user_id -> User (the observers)
        self._history: list[Message] = []        # ordered, append-only
        self._delivery = delivery                # how the fan-out is computed
        self._now = now_fn                       # injected clock (testable)
        self._next_id = id_fn                    # injected message-id factory

    def add_member(self, user: User) -> None:
        # WHY: a set-like guard — joining twice is a real error, not a silent
        #      no-op, because it usually signals a buggy caller.
        if user.id in self._members:
            raise AlreadyMemberError(
                f"user {user.id!r} is already a member of room {self.room_id!r}"
            )
        self._members[user.id] = user

    def remove_member(self, user_id: str) -> None:
        if user_id not in self._members:
            raise NotAMemberError(
                f"user {user_id!r} is not a member of room {self.room_id!r}"
            )
        del self._members[user_id]

    def is_member(self, user_id: str) -> bool:
        return user_id in self._members

    def accept(self, sender_id: str, text: str) -> tuple[Message, list[User]]:
        # WHY: this is the critical section the orchestrator runs UNDER the lock.
        #      It does the three things that must be consistent together —
        #      validate the sender, stamp+record the message, and snapshot the
        #      recipients — then hands the recipient list back so the caller can
        #      do the slow fan-out OUTSIDE the lock.
        if sender_id not in self._members:
            raise NotAMemberError(
                f"user {sender_id!r} is not a member of room {self.room_id!r}"
            )
        message = Message(
            id=self._next_id(),
            sender_id=sender_id,
            room_id=self.room_id,
            text=text,
            timestamp=self._now(),
        )
        self._history.append(message)
        # Snapshot recipients while we still hold the lock, so a join happening
        # right now is either fully in or fully out — never a torn read.
        recipients = self._delivery.select_recipients(
            self.room_id, sender_id, list(self._members.values())
        )
        return message, recipients

    def history(self) -> list[Message]:
        # WHY: hand back a COPY so a caller iterating the history can never see
        #      it grow underneath them, and can never mutate our source of truth.
        return list(self._history)


class ChatServer:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The orchestrator and public API. It owns the rooms map and the global user
    # directory, validates every request at the boundary, and delegates the
    # actual mediation to the room. The `_lock` hook is a no-op here; the
    # thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY that one method.
    def __init__(
        self,
        delivery: DeliveryStrategy | None = None,
        now_fn: Callable[[], float] = time.time,
    ) -> None:
        self._rooms: dict[str, ChatRoom] = {}
        self._users: dict[str, User] = {}        # every user the server has seen
        self._delivery = delivery or BroadcastToAll()
        self._now = now_fn
        self._msg_counter = itertools.count(1)   # numbered ids: msg-1, msg-2, ...

    # ── the no-op lock hook (Template Method) ──
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, room: ChatRoom) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: does nothing in the clean version — zero lock code in this class.
        #      ThreadSafeChatServer overrides this with a real per-room RLock.
        yield

    # ── public API ──
    def create_room(self, room_id: str) -> None:
        # WHY: idempotent on purpose — creating a room that exists is a safe
        #      no-op, so a racing "ensure the room is there" caller never errors.
        if room_id in self._rooms:
            return
        self._rooms[room_id] = ChatRoom(
            room_id, self._delivery, self._now, self._make_message_id
        )

    def join(self, room_id: str, user: User) -> None:
        room = self._require_room(room_id)
        with self._lock(room):
            room.add_member(user)                # raises AlreadyMemberError
        # Register globally so a later send() can tell "unknown user" from
        # "known user, wrong room". Idempotent: same id just overwrites.
        self._users[user.id] = user

    def leave(self, room_id: str, user_id: str) -> None:
        room = self._require_room(room_id)
        with self._lock(room):
            room.remove_member(user_id)          # raises NotAMemberError

    def send(self, room_id: str, sender_id: str, text: str) -> Message:
        room = self._require_room(room_id)       # UnknownRoomError
        self._require_known_user(sender_id)      # UnknownUserError
        with self._lock(room):
            message, recipients = room.accept(sender_id, text)   # NotAMemberError
        # WHY: fan out the observer callbacks OUTSIDE the lock — a slow receiver
        #      must never block another member from sending into the same room.
        for member in recipients:
            member.receive(message)
        return message

    def history(self, room_id: str) -> list[Message]:
        room = self._require_room(room_id)
        with self._lock(room):
            return room.history()

    # ── private helpers ──
    def _require_room(self, room_id: str) -> ChatRoom:
        room = self._rooms.get(room_id)
        if room is None:
            raise UnknownRoomError(f"no room with id {room_id!r}")
        return room

    def _require_known_user(self, user_id: str) -> None:
        if user_id not in self._users:
            raise UnknownUserError(
                f"no user with id {user_id!r} is known to the server"
            )

    def _make_message_id(self) -> str:
        # WHY: next() on itertools.count is atomic in CPython, and for one room
        #      it is also serialized by that room's lock — so ids never collide.
        return f"msg-{next(self._msg_counter)}"


class ThreadSafeChatServer(ChatServer):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The ONLY change for concurrency: override the `_lock` hook with a real
    # per-room reentrant lock. The clean core above keeps zero lock code.
    # 🎙️ "The diff from clean to thread-safe is one method. I keep one lock per
    #     room so ten different rooms never block each other; only members of the
    #     SAME room serialize, and that critical section is microseconds."
    def __init__(
        self,
        delivery: DeliveryStrategy | None = None,
        now_fn: Callable[[], float] = time.time,
    ) -> None:
        super().__init__(delivery, now_fn)
        self._room_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
        self._locks_guard = threading.Lock()     # guards the dict of locks

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, room: ChatRoom) -> Iterator[None]:
        # Double-lock idiom: take the small guard to fetch-or-create the room's
        # lock, then hold the room lock for the actual work.
        with self._locks_guard:
            lock = self._room_locks.get(room.room_id)
            if lock is None:
                lock = threading.RLock()
                self._room_locks[room.room_id] = lock
        with lock:
            yield

# Python 3.11+
"""Narrated demo driver for the Group Chat / Chat Room system.

Run: `python3 main.py`. Each case prints a one-line "Case N ... -> OK".
"""

import threading

from chat_service import ChatServer, ThreadSafeChatServer
from models import (
    AlreadyMemberError,
    NotAMemberError,
    UnknownRoomError,
    UnknownUserError,
    User,
)
from strategies import MutedAwareDelivery


def main() -> None:
    # ── Case 1: a member sends; everyone else receives, sender does NOT ──
    server = ChatServer()
    server.create_room("general")
    alice, bob, carol = User("u1", "Alice"), User("u2", "Bob"), User("u3", "Carol")
    for u in (alice, bob, carol):
        server.join("general", u)
    msg = server.send("general", "u1", "hello room")
    got = {bob.inbox[0].text, carol.inbox[0].text}
    print(f"Case 1: Alice sent {msg.text!r}; Bob+Carol inbox={got}; Alice inbox={alice.inbox}")
    assert got == {"hello room"}
    assert alice.inbox == [], "sender must not receive its own message"
    print("Case 1: a message reaches every OTHER member, never the sender -> OK")

    # ── Case 2: history is ordered and append-only ──
    server.send("general", "u2", "second")
    server.send("general", "u3", "third")
    texts = [m.text for m in server.history("general")]
    print(f"Case 2: history order = {texts}")
    assert texts == ["hello room", "second", "third"]
    print("Case 2: the room keeps an ordered message history -> OK")

    # ── Case 3: every validation path raises its own typed error ──
    errs: list[str] = []
    try:
        server.join("nope", User("u9", "Nine"))
    except UnknownRoomError:
        errs.append("UnknownRoom")
    try:
        server.join("general", alice)                 # already a member
    except AlreadyMemberError:
        errs.append("AlreadyMember")
    try:
        server.send("general", "ghost", "hi")          # never joined anything
    except UnknownUserError:
        errs.append("UnknownUser")
    server.create_room("private")
    try:
        server.send("private", "u1", "hi")             # known user, wrong room
    except NotAMemberError:
        errs.append("NotAMember")
    print(f"Case 3: typed errors fired = {errs}")
    assert errs == ["UnknownRoom", "AlreadyMember", "UnknownUser", "NotAMember"]
    print("Case 3: unknown room / dup join / unknown user / non-member all rejected -> OK")

    # ── Case 4: MutedAwareDelivery skips a member who muted the room ──
    muted_server = ChatServer(delivery=MutedAwareDelivery())
    muted_server.create_room("dev")
    dave, erin = User("u4", "Dave"), User("u5", "Erin")
    muted_server.join("dev", dave)
    muted_server.join("dev", erin)
    erin.mute("dev")                                   # Erin silences this room
    muted_server.send("dev", "u4", "ping")
    print(f"Case 4: Erin muted 'dev'; Erin inbox={erin.inbox}")
    assert erin.inbox == [], "muted member must not receive"
    print("Case 4: the delivery Strategy skips muted members -> OK")

    # ── Case 5: N members send at once on a thread-safe server, nothing lost ──
    n = 25
    ts = ThreadSafeChatServer()
    ts.create_room("storm")
    members = [User(f"m{i}", f"M{i}") for i in range(n)]
    for u in members:
        ts.join("storm", u)
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)

    def blast(idx: int) -> None:
        barrier.wait()                                 # all n fire at one instant
        ts.send("storm", f"m{idx}", f"from-{idx}")

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=blast, args=(i,)) for i in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()
    history_len = len(ts.history("storm"))
    inbox_sizes = {len(u.inbox) for u in members}
    print(f"Case 5: {n} concurrent sends; history={history_len}; every inbox={inbox_sizes}")
    assert history_len == n
    assert inbox_sizes == {n - 1}                       # each got all-but-own
    print("Case 5: concurrent sends in one room — no lost or duplicated messages -> OK")

    print("\nAll cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

import pathlib
import sys

# Put the workspace root on sys.path so `from models import ...` works in tests.
sys.path.insert(0, str(pathlib.Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent))

# Python 3.11+
"""pytest suite for the Group Chat / Chat Room system.

Covers: every public method's happy path, every typed exception, the
"not echoed to sender" rule, history ordering, both delivery strategies, and a
STRICT threading.Barrier concurrency proof (every member receives exactly the
messages from all OTHERS, none lost, none duplicated, history == total sent).
"""

import threading

import pytest

from chat_service import ChatRoom, ChatServer, ThreadSafeChatServer
from models import (
    AlreadyMemberError,
    Message,
    NotAMemberError,
    UnknownRoomError,
    UnknownUserError,
    User,
)
from strategies import BroadcastToAll, MutedAwareDelivery


# ── fixtures ──
@pytest.fixture
def server() -> ChatServer:
    s = ChatServer()
    s.create_room("r")
    return s


def _join(server: ChatServer, room: str, *ids: str) -> dict[str, User]:
    users = {uid: User(uid, uid.upper()) for uid in ids}
    for u in users.values():
        server.join(room, u)
    return users


# ── happy paths, one per public method ──
def test_create_room_is_idempotent():
    s = ChatServer()
    s.create_room("r")
    s.create_room("r")                       # second call is a safe no-op
    assert s.history("r") == []


def test_join_then_send_delivers_to_others_only(server):
    users = _join(server, "r", "a", "b", "c")
    msg = server.send("r", "a", "hi")
    assert isinstance(msg, Message) and msg.sender_id == "a"
    assert users["b"].inbox == [msg]
    assert users["c"].inbox == [msg]
    assert users["a"].inbox == []            # sender never gets an echo


def test_leave_stops_delivery(server):
    users = _join(server, "r", "a", "b")
    server.leave("r", "b")
    server.send("r", "a", "after you left")
    assert users["b"].inbox == []            # b is gone, gets nothing


def test_history_is_ordered_and_copied(server):
    _join(server, "r", "a", "b")
    server.send("r", "a", "one")
    server.send("r", "b", "two")
    hist = server.history("r")
    assert [m.text for m in hist] == ["one", "two"]
    hist.append("garbage")                   # mutating the copy must not leak
    assert len(server.history("r")) == 2


# ── typed exception paths ──
def test_join_unknown_room_raises():
    s = ChatServer()
    with pytest.raises(UnknownRoomError):
        s.join("ghost", User("a", "A"))


def test_duplicate_join_raises(server):
    a = User("a", "A")
    server.join("r", a)
    with pytest.raises(AlreadyMemberError):
        server.join("r", a)


def test_leave_non_member_raises(server):
    with pytest.raises(NotAMemberError):
        server.leave("r", "nobody")


def test_leave_unknown_room_raises():
    s = ChatServer()
    with pytest.raises(UnknownRoomError):
        s.leave("ghost", "a")


def test_send_unknown_room_raises():
    s = ChatServer()
    with pytest.raises(UnknownRoomError):
        s.send("ghost", "a", "hi")


def test_send_by_unknown_user_raises(server):
    with pytest.raises(UnknownUserError):
        server.send("r", "never-seen", "hi")


def test_send_by_non_member_raises(server):
    # 'a' is known to the server (joined room "r") but not a member of "other"
    _join(server, "r", "a")
    server.create_room("other")
    with pytest.raises(NotAMemberError):
        server.send("other", "a", "hi")


def test_history_unknown_room_raises():
    s = ChatServer()
    with pytest.raises(UnknownRoomError):
        s.history("ghost")


# ── delivery strategies ──
def test_broadcast_to_all_skips_only_sender():
    members = [User("a", "A"), User("b", "B"), User("c", "C")]
    out = BroadcastToAll().select_recipients("r", "a", members)
    assert {m.id for m in out} == {"b", "c"}


def test_muted_aware_skips_muted_and_sender():
    a, b, c = User("a", "A"), User("b", "B"), User("c", "C")
    b.mute("r")
    out = MutedAwareDelivery().select_recipients("r", "a", [a, b, c])
    assert {m.id for m in out} == {"c"}      # a is sender, b muted


def test_muted_member_gets_nothing_end_to_end():
    s = ChatServer(delivery=MutedAwareDelivery())
    s.create_room("r")
    a, b = User("a", "A"), User("b", "B")
    s.join("r", a)
    s.join("r", b)
    b.mute("r")
    s.send("r", "a", "ping")
    assert b.inbox == []


# ── mediator decoupling: a room never wires members to each other ──
def test_room_accept_returns_recipient_snapshot():
    a, b = User("a", "A"), User("b", "B")
    room = ChatRoom("r", BroadcastToAll(), lambda: 0.0, lambda: "m1")
    room.add_member(a)
    room.add_member(b)
    msg, recipients = room.accept("a", "hi")
    assert msg.id == "m1"
    assert [r.id for r in recipients] == ["b"]


# ── STRICT concurrency proof: Barrier => all senders race at one instant ──
def test_concurrent_sends_lose_no_messages():
    """N members each send once at the same instant into ONE room.

    Every member must end with EXACTLY the N-1 messages from all OTHERS — no
    losses, no duplicates — and the room history must hold all N messages.
    """
    n = 40
    ts = ThreadSafeChatServer()
    ts.create_room("storm")
    members = [User(f"m{i}", f"M{i}") for i in range(n)]
    for u in members:
        ts.join("storm", u)

    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)
    errors: list[Exception] = []

    def blast(idx: int) -> None:
        try:
            barrier.wait()                   # all n hit send() at one instant
            ts.send("storm", f"m{idx}", f"from-{idx}")
        except Exception as exc:             # noqa: BLE001 - record for assertion
            errors.append(exc)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=blast, args=(i,)) for i in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    assert errors == []                                  # no surprise failures
    history = ts.history("storm")
    assert len(history) == n                              # every send recorded
    assert len({m.id for m in history}) == n             # unique ids, no dup

    all_ids = {f"m{i}" for i in range(n)}
    for member in members:
        senders = {m.sender_id for m in member.inbox}
        assert len(member.inbox) == n - 1                # got all but its own
        assert len({m.id for m in member.inbox}) == n - 1  # no duplicate delivery
        assert senders == all_ids - {member.id}          # from everyone else


def test_concurrent_joins_during_broadcast_are_consistent():
    """Joins and sends racing in one room never corrupt membership or history."""
    n = 30
    ts = ThreadSafeChatServer()
    ts.create_room("mix")
    seed = User("seed", "Seed")
    ts.join("mix", seed)
    joiners = [User(f"j{i}", f"J{i}") for i in range(n)]
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)
    errors: list[Exception] = []

    def worker(i: int) -> None:
        try:
            barrier.wait()
            ts.join("mix", joiners[i])
            ts.send("mix", f"j{i}", f"hi-{i}")
        except Exception as exc:             # noqa: BLE001
            errors.append(exc)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(i,)) for i in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    assert errors == []
    # n joiners each sent exactly once => history holds n messages.
    assert len(ts.history("mix")) == n
    assert len({m.id for m in ts.history("mix")}) == n

blocks.md:
All tests pass. Here's the complete, interview-ready design.

> 🎙️ **Open with this framing** (the senior move for this problem): "A group chat is, at its core, a *decoupling* problem. The naive version wires every member to every other member — that's N-squared links and a maintenance nightmare. The whole design hinges on one idea: members never talk to each other. They talk to the **room** (the Mediator), and the room pushes each message into a one-method **observer** callback on every other member. Adding or removing a member becomes O(1) and touches nobody else."

## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan

**Interview mode: B — coding environment (Uber / Meta / startup style).** I write and RUN `main.py` + `pytest`. For a whiteboard round (Amazon / Google), skip §7's run and trace the cases verbally instead.

| Time | What you do | Mode |
|------|-------------|------|
| 0–5 min | **Say §1-2 out loud** — restate "group chat where the room mediates", ask clarifying questions, lock scope. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 5–8 min | **Pin the API surface (§3.5)** — five public calls on `ChatServer`: `create_room`, `join`, `leave`, `send`, `history`. "Before classes, here's the contract external code calls." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 8–14 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — `ChatServer`, `ChatRoom`, `User`, `Message`, `DeliveryStrategy`. Mention nothing simpler. | ✍️ DRAW |
| 14–16 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — Mediator + Observer + Strategy + Template Method, then immediately: "there's a check-then-act race between a `send` snapshotting members and a concurrent `join` — I'll fix it at the end." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 16–18 min | **Walk §5.5 approach** — the key insight (room mediates, observer delivers) + the `accept`-then-fan-out algorithm in plain words. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** (see §6.0): `Message` + `User` → `DeliveryStrategy` → `ChatRoom` (mediator) → `ChatServer` skeleton → `send()` → `join`/`leave`/`history`. | ✍️ WRITE |
| 38–45 min | **Demo Case 1 + Case 5** — a send fanning out to all-but-sender, then the concurrent-send proof. Run `main.py`. | 🗣️ SAY / run |
| 45–55 min | **Concurrency answer** — per-room `RLock` via the `_lock` hook; snapshot under lock, fan out outside. Write the subclass only if asked. | 🗣️ SAY (❓ code if asked) |
| 55–60 min | **Follow-ups** — distributed fan-out, 10M rooms, ordering guarantees (from §9). | ❓ IF ASKED |

**The one thing that makes interviewers flag you "strong hire":** Proactively say "I see a race condition between `send` and `join`" right after the diagram — don't wait to be asked.

---

## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions

### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)

We're building the core of a group chat — think a Slack channel or a WhatsApp group. People join a room, and when one of them types a message, everybody *else* in that room sees it (you don't get your own message echoed back). The room remembers everything said, in order. The hard constraint the problem keeps repeating is **decoupling**: a member should know about the room, not about the other members — so the system can add or drop members without rewiring anyone.

### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)

**You:** "Let me lock the core operations first. The v1 surface is: create a room, join, leave, send a message, and read history. That's it — no DMs, no threads/replies, no reactions. Sound right?"
**Interviewer:** "Yep, that's the set."
*✓ scoped: five operations, group rooms only — DMs are just a room of two later.*

**You:** "On `send`, I'll deliver to every *other* member — the sender doesn't receive their own message back. The room is the source of truth and keeps an ordered history. OK?"
**Interviewer:** "Exactly what I want."
*✓ 'not echoed to sender' is a hard rule that lives in the delivery policy.*

**You:** "I want delivery to be *pluggable* — default is broadcast-to-everyone, but I'll build a seam so 'skip muted members' or later 'skip offline' is a new class, not an `if` in the core. Worth doing now?"
**Interviewer:** "Yes, show me that seam."
*✓ this is the Strategy — I'll wire `BroadcastToAll` + `MutedAwareDelivery`.*

**You:** "For validation I'll reject: unknown room, sending by a non-member, and a duplicate join. I'll also distinguish 'I've never heard of this user' from 'I know this user but they're not in *this* room' — two different errors. Reasonable?"
**Interviewer:** "I like the distinction, keep it."
*✓ `UnknownUserError` (authenticate) vs `NotAMemberError` (authorize) — clean layering.*

**You:** "I'll treat delivery as in-process push — message presence, persistence to a DB, push notifications, and presence/typing indicators are all downstream and out of scope for this round. Agreed?"
**Interviewer:** "Agreed, keep it in-memory."
*✓ in-memory; the inbox is a list, not a network socket.*

**You:** "Last one — chat is genuinely concurrent (many people send and join at once). I'll design a clean single-threaded core, flag the race, and show a per-room lock at the end. That's the right shape for an interview?"
**Interviewer:** "Perfect, do exactly that."
*✓ clean core first; thread-safety is a one-method subclass via the lock hook.*

### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)

**Functional Requirements**

1. **Create a room** — `create_room(room_id)`.
   - Idempotent: creating a room that already exists is a safe no-op (lets a racing "ensure it exists" caller avoid errors).
2. **Join / leave** — a user joins or leaves a room.
   - `join` rejects an unknown room and a duplicate join with typed errors.
   - `leave` rejects an unknown room and a non-member with typed errors.
3. **Send** — a member sends text to a room; it is delivered to **all other members**, never the sender.
   - Returns the delivered `Message` (id, sender, room, text, timestamp).
   - Rejects unknown room, an unknown sender, and a sender who isn't a member.
4. **History** — each room keeps an **ordered, append-only** message history; `history(room_id)` returns a copy.
5. **Pluggable delivery policy** — how a message fans out is a Strategy: `BroadcastToAll` (default) and `MutedAwareDelivery` (skip members who muted the room).
6. **No member-to-member coupling** — members reference only the room (Mediator); delivery is a one-method observer callback (`User.receive`).
7. **Thread-safety** — concurrent sends and joins in the same room must not lose, duplicate, or corrupt messages or membership.

**Out of scope**

- Persistence / a real database (we keep history in memory — swapping in a repository is a later seam).
- Push notifications, presence, typing indicators (downstream systems — we just return the message).
- Direct messages, threads/replies, reactions, edits/deletes (a DM is a 2-person room; the rest are v2 features).
- AuthN/AuthZ beyond membership (who is allowed to join is an upstream concern).

> 💬 **What you say to open §2:** "Before I model anything, the one thing I want locked is the decoupling rule — members know the room, never each other. With that, let me walk the actors and flows."

---

## 2. Actors & Core Flows

**Actors**

- **Member (User):** a person in one or more rooms; sends messages and receives others' messages via a callback. The Observer.
- **ChatServer (orchestrator):** the public API; owns rooms and the global user directory, validates every request.
- **ChatRoom (mediator):** owns its members, history, and delivery policy; the only object that ever touches a member.
- **Delivery policy (system actor):** decides the recipient set for each message; swappable.

**Core flows**

1. Admin/user: `create_room("general")` → room exists, empty history.
2. Member: `join("general", alice)` → Alice is now wired to the room (and only the room).
3. Member: `send("general", alice, "hi")` → room records it → strategy picks recipients → each other member's `receive()` fires.
4. Member: `leave("general", "bob")` → Bob drops out; future sends skip him; O(1), nobody else notified-by-reference.
5. Anyone: `history("general")` → ordered copy of every message the room recorded.

---

## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)

### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough (accept / reject one by one)

> **ChatRoom** — A real thing with identity, a member set, an ordered history, and behavior (accept a message, route it). It's the Mediator — the hub every member points at. *Accepted — it's the heart of the design.*
>
> **User / Member** — Has identity, an inbox, and a callback (`receive`). It's the Observer side and it owns one preference (which rooms it muted). It has both state and behavior. *Accepted.*
>
> **Message** — Carries sender, room, text, and timestamp. It never changes once created, so it's a frozen value object — the same instance can safely sit in history AND in every inbox. *Accepted (as a value object).*
>
> **ChatServer** — The orchestrator / public API. Owns the rooms map and the global user directory; validates at the boundary. *Accepted.*
>
> **DeliveryStrategy** — Not a data entity, but a behavior we deliberately want to vary (broadcast vs mute-aware). It earns a class because the fan-out policy is exactly the thing the business will tweak. *Accepted (Strategy interface).*
>
> **Inbox** — Tempting to model as its own class. But an inbox is just "the list of messages a user has received" — it's a `list[Message]` field on `User` with no behavior of its own beyond append. *Rejected — a field on `User`, not an entity.*
>
> **Membership** — The "user X is in room Y" relationship. It feels like a join-table entity, but it carries no data beyond "is present", so it's just a key in the room's member map. *Rejected — modeled as `dict[user_id, User]` on the room.*
>
> **Mute / MutePreference** — "User X muted room Y." No lifecycle, no behavior — it's a `set[str]` of room ids on the `User`. *Rejected — a field on `User`, read by the strategy.*
>
> **Timestamp / Text / RoomId** — Primitive attributes of a `Message`, not entities. *Rejected — fields.*

### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)

| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ChatServer` | Public API; validate requests, own rooms + global users | `_rooms: dict[str, ChatRoom]` (id→room), `_users: dict[str, User]` (global directory), `_delivery: DeliveryStrategy` (injected) | Every public call validates room/user existence before mutating anything |
| `ChatRoom` | Mediator: own members, history, delivery; route one message | `room_id: str` *(id)*, `_members: dict[str, User]`, `_history: list[Message]`, `_delivery: DeliveryStrategy` | A message is recorded **and** its recipients snapshotted as one consistent step |
| `User` | Member + Observer: hold an inbox, receive via a callback | `id: str` *(id)*, `name: str`, `inbox: list[Message]`, `muted_rooms: set[str]` | Never references another `User` — only its own inbox |
| `Message` | Immutable record of one chat message | `id: str` *(id)*, `sender_id: str`, `room_id: str`, `text: str`, `timestamp: float` | Frozen — never mutated after creation |
| `DeliveryStrategy` | Decide the recipient set for a message | *(interface)* `select_recipients(room_id, sender_id, members)` | Every concrete strategy NEVER echoes to the sender |

**Enums:** none needed. The domain has no finite state vocabulary — membership is a set, history is a list, and delivery is a behavior, not a status. (A junior often reaches for a `MessageStatus` enum here; there's no state machine to justify it in v1.)

### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro (the natural-language story)

> "So the model has four objects plus one interface. `ChatServer` is the brain — it owns the rooms and a directory of every user it's seen, and it's the only thing that validates requests. `ChatRoom` is the interesting one: it's the **mediator**, the single hub each member points at, and it owns the members, the ordered history, and the delivery policy. `User` is the **observer** — it has an inbox and exactly one callback, `receive`; crucially it never holds a reference to another user. `Message` is a frozen value object so the same instance can live in history and in every inbox safely. And `DeliveryStrategy` is the seam for *who* gets a message. The things I deliberately left out — Inbox, Membership, Mute — those aren't entities; they're a list, a dict key, and a set, because none of them owns behavior."

---

## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)

This is the contract external code calls — `ChatServer`'s public methods only. Internal helpers and `ChatRoom`/`User` methods derive in §4.

| Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `create_room` | `(room_id: str) -> None` | — | — | Idempotently create a room (req 1) |
| `join` | `(room_id: str, user: User) -> None` | — | `UnknownRoomError`, `AlreadyMemberError` | A user joins a room and is wired only to it (req 2) |
| `leave` | `(room_id: str, user_id: str) -> None` | — | `UnknownRoomError`, `NotAMemberError` | A member leaves; future sends skip them (req 2) |
| `send` | `(room_id: str, sender_id: str, text: str) -> Message` | the delivered `Message` | `UnknownRoomError`, `UnknownUserError`, `NotAMemberError` | A member sends; it fans out to all others (req 3) |
| `history` | `(room_id: str) -> list[Message]` | ordered copy of the room's messages | `UnknownRoomError` | Read the room's append-only history (req 4) |

> 🎙️ "The whole system is five calls — two membership mutations (`join`/`leave`), one message in (`send`), one read (`history`), and an idempotent setup (`create_room`). Notice `send` returns the `Message` it built, and notice the *three* distinct errors on `send`: unknown room, unknown user, and known-but-not-a-member — that two-step 'who are you / are you allowed in here' is the layering I'd want even in v1. If you handed me this table cold, you'd know exactly what a group chat does without seeing one class."

Every method here is a public method on `ChatServer` in §4.1, appears in the §4.2 diagram, and is implemented in §6 — same name, same signature. The reverse holds too: `ChatServer` has no public method that isn't in this table.

---

## 4. Class Design (derive each class, then the diagram)

### 4.1 Per-class derivation (orchestrator first, then the parts)

#### `ChatServer` — the orchestrator

**(a) Role.** The system's public API. External code calls it to create rooms, manage membership, send, and read. Every request flows through here and is validated before any state changes.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "create rooms; route by room_id" | A map: room_id → `ChatRoom` |
| "distinguish unknown user from non-member" | A global directory: user_id → `User` |
| "pluggable delivery policy" | The injected `DeliveryStrategy` to hand each room |
| "stamp messages with ids + time" | A message-id counter and an injected clock |

```
class ChatServer:
    - _rooms: dict[str, ChatRoom]
    - _users: dict[str, User]
    - _delivery: DeliveryStrategy
    - _now: () -> float
    - _msg_counter: counter
```

*State placement trade-off.* The **member set and history are RELATIONAL to a room**, so they live on `ChatRoom`, not the server. The **global user directory is RELATIONAL to the whole system** (it answers "has the server ever seen this id?"), so it lives on the server. A `User`'s inbox and mute set are **physical to the user** (they describe that user's condition), so they live on `User`. That split is the whole Information-Expert story.

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "create rooms" | `create_room(room_id) -> None` |
| "users join / leave" | `join(room_id, user) -> None`, `leave(room_id, user_id) -> None` |
| "send to all others" | `send(room_id, sender_id, text) -> Message` |
| "ordered history" | `history(room_id) -> list[Message]` |
| "guard concurrency without polluting the core" | `_lock(room)` no-op hook |

```
class ChatServer:
    - _rooms: dict[str, ChatRoom]
    - _users: dict[str, User]
    - _delivery: DeliveryStrategy
    - _now: () -> float
    - _msg_counter: counter

    + ChatServer(delivery=None, now_fn=time.time)
    + create_room(room_id) -> None
    + join(room_id, user) -> None
    + leave(room_id, user_id) -> None
    + send(room_id, sender_id, text) -> Message
    + history(room_id) -> list[Message]
    # _lock(room) -> contextmanager            (no-op; overridden by ThreadSafe subclass)
    # _require_room(room_id) -> ChatRoom
    # _require_known_user(user_id) -> None
    # _make_message_id() -> str
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out.**
- **Why does `send` return the `Message`?** Callers want the stamped id/timestamp for their own correlation (e.g., to render or to ack), so we return the object the room built rather than `None`.
- **Why a global `_users` directory separate from room membership?** It lets `send` distinguish "this id was never seen" (`UnknownUserError`) from "known user, wrong room" (`NotAMemberError`). That's authenticate-then-authorize.
- **Why is `_lock` a method, not inline locking?** The clean core has zero lock code; the thread-safe version overrides exactly this one method (Template Method). The diff is ~10 lines.

#### `ChatRoom` — the mediator

**(a) Role.** The Mediator. It's the single hub each member points at; it owns membership, the ordered history, and the delivery policy, and it's the only object that ever touches a member.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "users join/leave a room" | A map: user_id → `User` (the observers) |
| "ordered message history" | An append-only `list[Message]` |
| "pluggable fan-out" | The `DeliveryStrategy` to ask for recipients |
| "stamp messages" | Injected clock + message-id factory |

```
class ChatRoom:
    - room_id: str
    - _members: dict[str, User]
    - _history: list[Message]
    - _delivery: DeliveryStrategy
    - _now: () -> float
    - _next_id: () -> str
```

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "join" | `add_member(user) -> None` (raises `AlreadyMemberError`) |
| "leave" | `remove_member(user_id) -> None` (raises `NotAMemberError`) |
| "send: record + pick recipients atomically" | `accept(sender_id, text) -> (Message, list[User])` (raises `NotAMemberError`) |
| "ordered history copy" | `history() -> list[Message]` |
| "membership check" | `is_member(user_id) -> bool` |

```
class ChatRoom:
    - room_id: str
    - _members: dict[str, User]
    - _history: list[Message]
    - _delivery: DeliveryStrategy
    - _now: () -> float
    - _next_id: () -> str

    + ChatRoom(room_id, delivery, now_fn, id_fn)
    + add_member(user) -> None
    + remove_member(user_id) -> None
    + is_member(user_id) -> bool
    + accept(sender_id, text) -> tuple[Message, list[User]]
    + history() -> list[Message]
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out.**
- **Why does `accept` return `(Message, recipients)` instead of delivering itself?** So the orchestrator can do the slow fan-out (the observer callbacks) **outside** the lock. `accept` is the consistent critical section: validate sender, stamp+record, snapshot recipients — all under one lock; delivery happens after.
- **Why snapshot recipients *inside* `accept`?** A concurrent `join` must be either fully in or fully out of this message — snapshotting under the lock prevents a torn read.
- **Why does the room own the `DeliveryStrategy`, not the server?** The room *is* the mediator; "how do I fan out" is the room's job. The server just injects which policy.

#### `User` — the member / observer

**(a) Role.** A member and the Observer side. It exposes one delivery callback (`receive`) and owns its own preferences. It never references another user.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "receive messages from others" | An `inbox: list[Message]` |
| "skip-muted delivery policy" | A `muted_rooms: set[str]` |
| "identity" | `id`, `name` |

```
class User:
    + id: str
    + name: str
    + inbox: list[Message]
    + muted_rooms: set[str]
```

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "delivery is an observer callback" | `receive(message) -> None` |
| "a user can mute/unmute a room" | `mute(room_id) -> None`, `unmute(room_id) -> None` |

```
class User:
    + id: str
    + name: str
    + inbox: list[Message]
    + muted_rooms: set[str]

    + receive(message) -> None      # the one observer callback
    + mute(room_id) -> None
    + unmute(room_id) -> None
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out.**
- **Why is `receive` just an append?** It's the observer hook; keeping it dumb means the room (mediator) owns all routing logic, and the append is atomic under the GIL so concurrent deliveries don't collide.
- **Why does mute live on `User`, not `ChatRoom`?** Muting is a per-user preference; storing it on the user means the room holds zero mute state and the strategy reads it off each candidate.

#### `Message` — the value object

**(a) Role.** An immutable record of one chat message — the unit that lives in history and in inboxes.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "who sent it, to which room, what, when" | `sender_id`, `room_id`, `text`, `timestamp` |
| "identity for dedup/ordering" | `id` |

```
class Message:   # frozen
    + id: str
    + sender_id: str
    + room_id: str
    + text: str
    + timestamp: float
```

**(c) Operations derivation.** None — it's a pure value object; it carries data and is never mutated.

```
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Message:
    + id: str
    + sender_id: str
    + room_id: str
    + text: str
    + timestamp: float
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out.**
- **Why frozen?** The same instance sits in the room's history and in every recipient's inbox. Freezing guarantees no reader can mutate what another reader sees.
- **Why no methods?** All behavior (who gets it) belongs to the strategy and the room — the message is inert data on purpose.

#### `DeliveryStrategy` — the fan-out seam

**(a) Role.** The interface the room depends on to decide *who* receives a message. The extensibility seam.

**(b) State derivation.** None — strategies are stateless behavior objects.

```
interface DeliveryStrategy:
    (no state)
```

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method |
|---|---|
| "broadcast / skip-muted / (later) skip-offline" | `select_recipients(room_id, sender_id, members) -> list[User]` |

```
interface DeliveryStrategy:
    + select_recipients(room_id, sender_id, members) -> list[User]

class BroadcastToAll(DeliveryStrategy):
    + select_recipients(...)  # everyone except sender

class MutedAwareDelivery(DeliveryStrategy):
    + select_recipients(...)  # everyone except sender and muters
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out.**
- **Why an interface for two implementations?** Because "who gets a message" is precisely what the business tweaks (muted today, online-only tomorrow). The contract — "never the sender" — is the one rule every concrete keeps.
- **Why pass `members` in rather than reading them off the room?** It keeps the strategy a pure function of its inputs — trivially unit-testable with a hand-built list, no room needed.

**Relationships, one edge at a time:**
- **`ChatServer <|-- ThreadSafeChatServer` (inheritance):** the thread-safe server *is a* `ChatServer` that overrides only `_lock`. Solid arrow = extends.
- **`ChatServer *-- ChatRoom` (composition, 1 → 0..*):** the server *owns* its rooms; kill the server and the rooms go with it. Filled diamond.
- **`ChatServer o-- User` (aggregation, 1 → 0..*):** the server holds a directory of users it has seen, but it doesn't own their lifetime — the same `User` exists independently and across rooms. Open diamond.
- **`ChatRoom o-- User` (aggregation, 1 → 0..*):** a room references its members but doesn't own them — a user outlives membership and can be in many rooms. This is the link that replaces N-squared member-to-member wiring.
- **`ChatRoom --> DeliveryStrategy` (association, 1 → 1):** the room holds the policy it asks for recipients. **This is the extensibility SEAM.**
- **`ChatServer ..> DeliveryStrategy` (dependency):** the server injects which concrete strategy each room uses, but depends only on the abstraction.
- **`ChatRoom ..> Message` (dependency):** the room *creates* messages; it doesn't store the class, it instantiates it.
- **`User o-- Message` (aggregation, 1 → 0..*):** each user's inbox holds references to messages (the same frozen instances that are in history).

**Pattern Map**

| Class / Interface | Pattern | One-line reason |
|---|---|---|
| `ChatRoom` | Mediator | Members talk to the room, never each other — collapses N-squared links into N |
| `User.receive` | Observer | The room pushes each message via one callback; the user is a pure subscriber |
| `DeliveryStrategy` | Strategy | The room depends on the abstraction; broadcast vs mute-aware is a swap |
| `ChatServer._lock` hook | Template Method | No-op in the clean core; `ThreadSafeChatServer` overrides the one method |
| `Message` | Value Object | Frozen, immutable, freely shared between history and inboxes |

Hanging off the room on the right is `DeliveryStrategy` — the Strategy seam; `BroadcastToAll` and `MutedAwareDelivery` implement it, each a one-method class. `User` sits below with its inbox of `Message`s, and `Message` is the frozen value object. The key seam is `DeliveryStrategy`: the room never knows which concrete it's asking, so a new fan-out rule plugs in as a new class with zero room changes. It's a classic check-then-act / read-write race on shared state. I'll keep the core clean and single-threaded for now and show the per-room `RLock` fix in §9 — snapshot under the lock, fan out outside it."

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

### 5.1 Information Expert (state this first)

> "I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to its data. `ChatRoom` owns membership, history, and routing because it's the only thing that sees all of a room's members — so it's the right expert to record a message and decide who gets it. `User` owns its inbox and mute set because those describe the user's own condition. `ChatServer` owns the rooms map and the global user directory because it's the only thing that sees the whole system. `Message` owns nothing but its data — it's inert by design. That ownership split is *why* the patterns below fall out naturally rather than being bolted on."

### 5.2 Applied patterns (each one earned)

**Mediator — `ChatRoom`.**
- **Why it fits here:** The problem's hard constraint is "no member-to-member coupling." Without a mediator, every member would hold references to every other member — N-squared links, and every join/leave would have to rewire everyone. The room *is* the mediator: each member knows only the room, the room knows the members.
- **Seam:** `ChatRoom.accept()` is where members are coordinated without knowing each other.
- 🎙️ "The room is a mediator — members never reference each other; they all go through the room."
- 💬 **Layman:** "It's like a group email list. You don't keep everyone's email address; you send to the list, and the list delivers. Add or remove a person and nobody else changes anything."

**Observer — `User.receive`.**
- **Why it fits here:** Delivery should be push, and the room shouldn't care what a member *does* with a message. So each member is a subscriber exposing one callback; the room (subject) notifies subscribers.
- **Seam:** `for member in recipients: member.receive(message)` in `ChatServer.send`.
- 🎙️ "Delivery is Observer — the room pushes each message into a one-method callback; members are pure subscribers."
- 💬 **Layman:** "Each person has a mailbox slot. The room just drops the message in the slot — it never asks what you'll do with it."

**Strategy — `DeliveryStrategy`.**
- **Why it fits here:** The fan-out rule is exactly what the business will vary — broadcast today, skip-muted now, skip-offline later. Extracting it means the room never hardcodes the policy.
- **Interface + concretes:** `DeliveryStrategy.select_recipients(...)` → `BroadcastToAll`, `MutedAwareDelivery`.
- 🎙️ "Who-gets-a-message is a Strategy; the room asks the policy and never knows which one is wired in."
- 💬 **Layman:** "The room doesn't decide the rule for who hears you — it asks a rule-of-the-day, so we can swap the rule without touching the room."

**Template Method — `ChatServer._lock`.**
- **Why it fits here:** I want the clean core to have zero lock code and the thread-safe version to differ by one method. `_lock` is a no-op placeholder the subclass overrides.
- **Seam:** `ThreadSafeChatServer._lock` overrides the base no-op with a per-room `RLock`.
- 🎙️ "Concurrency is a Template Method — a placeholder lock the thread-safe subclass replaces."
- 💬 **Layman:** "The core leaves a blank where a lock could go; the safe version fills the blank. Nothing else moves."

### 5.3 SOLID principles (specific)

- **Single Responsibility:** `ChatRoom` mediates, `User` subscribes, `DeliveryStrategy` selects recipients, `ChatServer` coordinates. Four reasons to change, four classes.
- **Open/Closed:** a new fan-out rule is a new `DeliveryStrategy` subclass — zero edits to `ChatRoom` or `ChatServer`.
- **Liskov:** `ThreadSafeChatServer` is a drop-in `ChatServer`; every test that passes against the base passes against the subclass (it only narrows timing, never behavior).
- **Dependency Inversion:** `ChatRoom` depends on the `DeliveryStrategy` abstraction, never on `BroadcastToAll` directly; the server injects the concrete.

### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS

Every pattern above is visible in §6 code with a real seam:
- Mediator → `ChatRoom.accept` is the only place members are coordinated; no `User` references another `User` anywhere (`Cmd-F` proves it).
- Observer → a real `User.receive` callback, fired in a loop in `send`.
- Strategy → a real ABC with two implementations the server injects.
- Template Method → a real `_lock` no-op overridden by `ThreadSafeChatServer`.

I am **not** claiming Singleton (there's no enforced single instance), Factory (no enum→object translation; `create_room` is plain construction), or Repository (history is in-memory; the repository is an *extension point, not wired in v1* — I'll name where it would go in §9).
